rationale: This reaction is a quintessential textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (H2SO4) conditions. This carbocation is then trapped by the simplest liquid nitrile, acetonitrile, to form a nitrilium ion intermediate, which upon aqueous workup hydrolyzes to the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and the product are simple, acyclic, and commercially available, making this a clear and unambiguous representation of the reaction family.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, saturated aliphatic compounds (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (Prins, Markovnikov addition), carbonyls (aldol, etc.), or other complex functional groups that could trigger incorrect family classifications. The core transformation is clearly the C-N bond formation between a carbocation source and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an amide, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
